data.title: SDCI: A Commodities Fund With Momentum
data.description:
Thesis

USCF SummerHaven Dynamic Commodity Strategy No K-1 Fund ETF (SDCI) is a commodities exchange-traded fund. The name employs an interesting strategy in selecting its holdings via the SummerHaven methodology, and represents a good choice for investors wanting to avoid a K-1 form.

The ETF follows the SummerHaven Dynamic Commodity Index, an index that uses fundamental signals about underlying physical markets to create an active benchmark for commodity futures investors. As per its website, the index:

Tracks the performance of a fully collateralized portfolio of 14 commodity futures, selected each month from a universe of 27 eligible commodities based on observable price signals, subject to a diversification requirement across major commodity sectors. The commodity sectors for the SDCI include Precious Metals, Industrial Metals, Energy and agricultural products such as Livestock, Softs, and Grains.

In today's article we are going to look at the index and fund composition, its momentum factors and performance, and outline why eliminating a K-1 form is a boon for retail investors.

No K-1 forms - a massive benefit

Many ETFs that invest in commodities end up being characterized for tax purposes as commodity pools, which means they hold futures contracts directly. The IRS classifies these pools as partnerships, which are "pass-through" entities. As a partnership, the fund doesn't pay its own income taxes. Instead, it passes through all its income, gains, losses, and expenses directly to its shareholders. The K-1 is the form that reports your share of these items, and you must use it to file your personal tax return.

K-1 forms are cumbersome, confusing and in many instances require professional tax personnel to ensure they get filed correctly. SDCI does not require investors to file a K-1, having used an innovative structure to allow it to issue the standard form 1099.

Many retail investors are loath to complete K-1 forms (this author included), and the fact that SDCI offers commodities exposure without a K-1 is a massive marketing and structuring win.

Index construction

The SummerHaven Dynamic Commodity Index is an interesting one, aiming to capture almost the entire commodities universe via its composition:

27 contracts are initially contemplated, with SDCI selecting only 14, taking into account the ones with the greatest backwardation. The selection process occurs each month, with each contract being equally weighted and representing the five sectors outlined above. We find it strange that the index quants have included Natural Gas in the 'Other' category rather than include it with the petroleum products, since natural gas is a by-product of oil extraction, and there is a fairly good long-term correlation between the prices of oil and natural gas, although shorter time-frame divergences can indeed occur.

The index requires at least one component from each of the four commodity sectors as outlined in the above table ('Precious Metals,' 'Industrial Metals,' 'Petroleum,' and 'Grains'). If a sector isn't represented after selecting the 14 commodities, the commodity with the highest backwardation among the commodities of the omitted sector is substituted for the commodity with the lowest backwardation among the 14 selected commodities. By taking this approach, the index composition does not consistently exclude any of the large four sectors.

The methodology for the index involves choosing 14 commodities with the greatest backwardation (or least contango). Backwardation is measured as the annualized percent price difference between the futures price for the closest-to-expiration contract and the next closest-to-expiration contract for each commodity. In essence, the index construction bets that the 'normal' curve for commodities' futures is contango; thus, backwardation will correct itself.

Current fund holdings

As per the above methodology, the fund currently runs 14 commodity exposures, which are as follows as of the writing of this article:

  • GOLD 100 OZ FUTR DEC25 (GCZ5)
  • LEAN HOGS FUTURE OCT25 (LHV5)
  • GASOLINE RBOB FUT Nov25 (XBX5)
  • LME TIN FUTURE Oct25 (LTV5)
  • COPPER FUTURE DEC25 (HGZ5)
  • WTI CRUDE FUTURE NOV25 (CLX5)
  • NY HARB ULSD FUT DEC25 (HOZ5)
  • BRENT CRUDE FUTR DEC25 (COZ5)
  • Low Su Gasoil G Dec25 (QSZ5)
  • LME ZINC FUTURE Oct25 (LXV5)
  • LIVE CATTLE FUTR OCT25 (LCV5)
  • LME PRI ALUM FUTR Oct25 (LAV25)
  • SOYBEAN MEAL FUTR Dec25 (SMZ5)
  • COFFEE 'C' FUTURE DEC25 (KCZ5)

Each of the above commodities represents roughly 7% of the fund, with variations due to mark-to-market until the next rebalancing date for the index and fund.

Performance

The name has had a very robust performance in the past year, beating its peers:

We are looking at a cohort composed of the Invesco DB Commodity Index Tracking Fund ETF (DBC), the Invesco Optimum Yield Diversified Commodity Strategy No K-1 ETF (PDBC) and the Direxion Auspice Broad Commodity Strategy ETF (COM). SDCI has outperformed, being up +20%, while COM has fared the worst, down -1%.

Historically the fund has also posted robust annual returns, with only a few negative years:

The name was down in 2020 (unsurprisingly) and down again in 2023. To note the outstanding performance in 2022 when oil prices spiked. Despite the robust annual returns, one cannot become complacent. The ETF is fairly volatile with a 11% 3-year standard deviation.

Momentum

The name comes on the back of a very strong performance in the past year, and with a large momentum factor behind it. The Seeking Alpha platform gives it a 'A' rating for momentum on their dedicated tab. For a fund like SDCI, this translates into its backwardation strategy working in today's macro environment.

Given that the ETF itself has its own selection methodology in place, the price action tells us that the economic environment lends itself to a normal cycle, where commodities in backwardation resolve themselves via the curve moving back into contango.

Some analytics

Let us also have a look at some of the fund's analytics:

  • AUM: $0.2 bil
  • Expense ratio: 0.6%
  • SEC yield: 3%

The manager has a subsidy in place to lower the expense ratio, and we believe it will continue to be at this level with subsequent discounts.

Conclusion

SDCI is a commodities ETF that does not use the dreaded K-1 form via its structuring acumen. The name follows the SummerHaven Dynamic Commodity Index, and in essence distills a universe of 27 commodities into 14 chosen ones. The methodology involves looking at backwardation on the curve and betting on a 'normalization' to contango. The fund has done very well in the past year, being up over +20%, and its current momentum rating is very high. We like what SDCI is doing and favor this name in today's macro environment where inflation seems to reassess itself higher.

data.title: The Only 2 Places I'd Trust With My Retirement Money Right Now
data.description:
Introduction

Is there anything better than holding stocks for the long term?

Over the past 35 years, the S&P 500 has had positive annual returns in eight of every ten years. That's an 80% hit rate if you were to buy stocks on the first day of every year and sell them on the last day. Professionals in other areas, like sports betting, come nowhere near that, as even Billy Walters, who many consider the best sports bettor in history, has a win rate of 58%.

All you had to do to beat the pros was buy an index fund. That's not hard.

Truly, the funny thing about long-term investing is that doing nothing is the biggest challenge for most.

  • Stocks rally 10%? Do nothing.
  • Stocks fall 10%? Do nothing.
  • The candidate you cannot stand wins the presidency? Do nothing.
  • A pandemic is quickly spreading. Do nothing.

To use findings from U.S. Bank, where I got the 80% stat above, there are at least five major benefits to passive investing:

  • Markets tend to recover over the long term. As the S&P 500 is currently close to an all-time high, we can say that any point in history turned out to be a good time to invest.
  • If you don't do anything, you won't miss out on the market's best days.
  • By not selling, one can faster recoup unrealized losses.
  • Without a long-term mindset, it is very hard to benefit from compounding interest.
  • Long-term investors enjoy dividends as part of the total return.

While most of this speaks for itself, point two is one of my favorites, as it is the cornerstone of an investor's commitment to long-term gains. The other day, I had a talk with a family member of mine. Someone who's excellent at his job but new when it comes to investing. He mentioned he had savings and wanted to finally try this "ETF thing" everyone keeps bringing up.

Once I told him about passive investing, he asked me if it's possible to include a stop loss to protect his wealth once stocks go south, like a hard stop loss at -15%.

Technically speaking, it's perfectly normal to come up with this idea.

Unfortunately, it's one of the best ways to destroy wealth, as dramatic as that may sound, as downside protection is a lie that could become very costly.

As we can see below, the stock market's best days are usually during bear markets when relief rallies tend to be violent. That's why both 2008 and 2020 are so prominent in the table below, which shows the best days in 1999-2024.

Now, let's say we sell during a market correction. Odds are we miss these days. It means we (very likely) have to buy our way back into the market at much more unfavorable prices. Using U.S. Bank data, missing the best ten days since 1990 would have resulted in an annual return of 7.7%, way below the 10.2% return that investors who never sold enjoyed. That's a big deal!

If you had even worse timing, odds are you would have barely beaten bonds.

This holds true going back much further than the 1990s as well:

Needless to say, sitting and doing nothing is hard. Very hard. I remember when I got serious about investing in 2020. Especially in early 2020, we simply didn't know how bad the pandemic could get. It was quite scary.

But it's the price we pay, as the market usually experiences a double-digit decline each year, as we can see below. More often than not, it still manages to end the year with a positive return. Since the Great Financial Crisis, most of these returns have been double-digit gains.

That's why everyone who invested $100,000 in the S&P 500 in 1993 had $600,000 after 20 years and roughly $1.8 million after 30 years.

Essentially, the hardest thing in long-term investing is getting the capital to invest and sticking to one's strategy. If history is any indication, you'll end up in a much better financial spot if you do these two things.

It becomes much more complex when picking one's own stocks instead of simply following an index. As this website is called Seeking Alpha, and I know that the overwhelming majority of my regular readers pick their own stocks, there are a lot of factors that apply to them that don't apply to ETF investors.

The biggest risks are single-company exposures. While the fact that the top ten stocks in the S&P 500 make up about 40% of its weight increases these risks for index investors, especially given Big Tech's reliance on AI, individual stock pickers depend on their ability to choose the right stocks.

Especially in the time of AI, I believe the market is massively underestimating the disruptive power of this new technology. I truly believe the majority of public companies are facing some form of disruption, some more than others.

[...] executives’ biggest concern is disruption. They worry that their companies will go the way of Blockbuster or AOL if their leaders aren’t quick enough to respond to emerging technologies. In a 2024 survey conducted by MIT Technology Review, 60% of respondents agreed that “generative AI technology will substantially disrupt our industry over the next five years.” - MIT Sloan

AI innovation is accelerating so fast that some call it a "super-acceleration" period, which makes it likely that AI will soon be smarter than the smartest humans. Just imagine what this means for the labor market, automation, new product development in areas like healthcare and engineering, and so many other factors. It's both exciting and scary, if you ask me.

AI's rapid advancement is caused by three factors: raw compute increases, algorithmic efficiency improvements, and "unhobbling" processes. In the current super-accelerated decade, we enjoy a 10,000x AI performance boost every four years, making it likely that AI will exceed top human experts by 2027. There are good arguments that AI progress past 2027 could be slower, but also that it could be even faster. UX Tigers (2024)

Anyway, I am not turning this into an AI article. I will go into much more depth to discuss the disruptive power of AI in future articles. I'm bringing this up because the margin of error is increasingly small when it comes to picking individual stocks. Especially for retired investors who depend on the income from their investments, reducing disruption risks is essential.

I will present you with two areas that are as disruption-proof as it gets. These areas would get a lot of my capital, as I believe they are perfect for retirement investors and anyone looking to buy high-quality income for whatever reason.

Needless to say, these two ideas will include a lot of food for thought.

Buying Real Estate And Energy Infrastructure

I'm not kidding when I say that real estate and energy infrastructure would get a lot of my capital if I were investing for income instead of a high total return. In fact, I like these areas, in general, as I own both REITs and midstream companies to improve the risk/reward of my portfolio.

REITs, which own and manage physical real estate assets like residential properties, retail buildings, and data centers, are largely protected against AI disruption because their value is tied to tangible, location-specific assets that AI cannot easily replace.

Or to put it differently, humans need buildings for everyday activities, including buying groceries, washing our cars, going to the office, refilling our prescriptions, going to the movies, getting a haircut, enjoying a bit with family and friends, and because we all need a roof over our heads.

In fact, and this is the cool part(!), AI is more likely to improve REIT operations through tools like predictive maintenance and tenant analytics than to threaten them.

For example, imagine you're Realty Income (O), which yields close to 5.5%, has hiked its monthly dividend for more than 25 consecutive years, and manages roughly 15,600 assets across the entire United States and Europe. While AI won't create a sudden surge in income, it can make life much easier for this corporation when it comes to monitoring its assets, finding weak spots, and exploiting opportunities. After all, in real estate, it's all about finding opportunities that yield more than the cost of capital.

In general, I believe net lease REITs with a focus on essential business tenants like Realty Income (see below), NNN REIT, W.P. Carey (WPC), Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT), Alpine Income Property Trust (PINE), and others, are in a great spot to defy any potential risks from AI. As worried as I may be for other areas, I think these companies remain the place to be.

Even better, Cohen & Steers made the case that AI has a minimal impact on REITs, in general, noting that the biggest weaknesses are higher e-commerce penetration for retail-focused REITs, job displacement that could pressure employment, income growth, and other factors supporting the housing market, as well as the impact of AI on office jobs.

I agree with that.

As I dislike the office market, I would make the case that everyone looking for opportunities in what may be the most hated real estate market should focus on players operating in high-tech areas with the right assets to attract AI beneficiaries. I believe Kilroy Realty Corp. (KRC) is one of these stocks.

However, given that it barely yields more than 5.0%, it's not a company I am getting super excited about. While it has a very low payout ratio of 63% (safe dividend) and trades at just 12.1x adjusted funds from operations ("AFFO"), analysts expect contracting per-share AFFO through at least 2027.

In residential real estate, I believe diversified players like Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA), Camden Property Trust (CPT), Equity LifeStyle Properties (ELS), and Invitation Homes (INVH). The first two are multi-family giants that most Americans may be familiar with. In the two weeks I spent in Florida earlier this year, I saw countless properties, often high-quality assets in high-demand areas occupied by people with good incomes.

Equity LifeStyle Properties owns manufactured housing properties, RV communities, and marinas, with most MH communities being reserved for retired homeowners, which substantially reduces financial risks.

Inviation Homes owns single-family homes in attractive markets.

When it comes to data centers, I like the two biggest players, Equinix (EQIX) and Digital Realty Trust (DLR). However, I have not covered them in a long time, as I prefer to invest in data centers through suppliers like Comfort Systems USA (FIX) and other hardware providers.

Over the past three years, which include the successful launch of ChatGPT, these two REITs have both failed to beat the S&P 500, which is somewhat of a red flag. While both have consistent growth rates, I believe the market knows that, at some point in the future, we'll likely see a data center glut.

On top of that, I like VICI Properties (VICI), which owns key assets on the Las Vegas strip and yields 5.1% with consistent single-digit annual growth.

My point is that I would go overweight in net lease retail, diversified multi-family, and select industrial REITs like Rexford Industrial (REXR) and Prologis (PLD), which use AI to their benefit and cannot be disrupted due to "mission-critical" assets that make the world go round, so to speak.

Although some REITs have had somewhat disappointing returns, I believe focusing on REITs is a very smart move going forward, as it's one of the very few sources of bullet-proof income for people who really need it.

And, truth be told, given that I will likely be entirely dependent on my own wealth once I retire, I'm not someone who takes this lightly.

Similarly, midstream companies, which operate pipelines, storage, and transportation/processing for oil and gas, rely on mission-critical infrastructure that supports AI instead of being disrupted by it. Kinder Morgan (KMI), for example, which moves roughly 40% of U.S. natural gas production, estimates a demand increase of nearly 30 billion cubic feet per day by the early 2030s.

As existing infrastructure has high utilization rates, this bodes well for midstream pricing power and returns of new projects.

Moreover, AI-driven improvements in logistics, emissions monitoring, and predictive analytics actually improve efficiency without undermining the core demand for energy, making midstream companies one of the biggest net winners of the AI boom.

Personally, I own Antero Midstream (AM), which yields close to 5.0%. Just like KMI, it's a C-Corp, meaning it does not issue a K-1 form. While its operations are mainly tied to supporting the processing and water capabilities of its parent, Antero Resources (AR), it sees huge data center opportunities in Appalachia, where 100% of its operations are located.

Another C-Corp I really like is TC Energy (TRP), the Canadian natural gas midstream giant that spun off its entire oil business. The company owns natural gas pipelines in all North American countries, the Bruce Power nuclear facility, and growth projects that support its 4.7% yield.

With that said, if you don't mind partnerships that issue K-1 forms, you may like Entergy Transfer (ET). This MLP has a strong natural gas focus and is in talks with a large number of data center companies to construct new assets directly on or in the vicinity of its pipeline network.

Needless to say, there are many other good companies. For now, my point is that both sectors offer attractive yields, often 4-6% or higher, derived from stable cash flows like rents or toll-like fees, making them ideal for retirement portfolios aiming to minimize AI-related volatility.

While I would never actually do it, if I had to for whatever weird reason, I truly believe I could build a stellar retirement portoflio using only REITs and midstream.

In fact, I think it wouldn't even be that hard, as both places offer tremendous qualities that could allow investors to buy both high-quality income and a real shot at beating what has become a rather expensive market.

Takeaway

As we discussed in the introduction, the hardest part of investing is also the easiest part, which is doing nothing. For that, the data is overwhelming.

  • Over the past 35 years, 80% of the annual S&P 500 returns were positive. It's hard to beat these odds in any other area, like gambling.
  • The biggest risk isn't a market crash. The biggest risk is what goes on between our ears. For example, selling during a downturn to protect wealth is a logical instinct. However, it's almost always a costly mistake, as it tends to result in missing the best days.

Now, for those of us who like picking individual stocks, the margin for error is shrinking in an AI-disrupted world. That's why, for bullet-proof income, I would put my capital in real estate and energy infrastructure.

Their tangible, mission-critical assets aren't just safe from severe disruption risks, but they even benefit from it. The cherry on top is that both areas are offering stable 4-8% yields from essential cash flows.

I have said it before, and I will say it again. These are two of the best areas to buy income right now, and I expect many of the picks mentioned in this article to fit a wide range of retirement or income-focused portfolios.

Risks To My Thesis

Every good thing on the market always comes with risks.

In this case, rapidly rising interest rates could pressure REITs and midstream valuations. Meanwhile, a severe, prolonged economic downturn could reduce demand for real estate space and energy. As safe as the stocks in this article may be, none of them are immune to (temporary) stock price weakness if these sectors go south.

data.title: Dollar Slumps Ahead Of The Employment Report
data.description:
Overview

The focus is on US employment today. Weak jobs growth and a tick up in the unemployment rate are expected to spur the Fed's first rate cut of the year in a couple of weeks. Position-adjusting ahead of the report has weighed on the greenback (DXY) broadly and overwhelmed the unexpectedly poor Germany factory orders and the data problem in the UK that has exaggerated activity. Only a handful of emerging market currencies have not been lifted by the selling pressure on the greenback. These include the Turkish lira, Russian ruble, and Indian rupee.

Stocks and bonds are firm. All the large bourses in the Asia-Pacific region rallied, led by the nearly 2.2% surge in China's CSI 300. The Nikkei, Hang Seng, and Taiwan's TAIEX each rose more than 1%. Europe's STOXX 600 (STOXX) came into today practically flat on the week and is up about 0.2%. US index futures are trading with a firmer bias as well. Gains in the S&P (SPX) and Nasdaq (NDX) will challenge the record highs. European benchmark 10-year yields are mostly softer, and premiums over Germany have narrowed a little. The 10-year US yield (US10Y) is a little lower, slipping through 4.16%. It is the lowest since May 1. Lower yields and a weaker dollar are helping support gold, which is hovering near $3350. It is around $100 higher on the week. October WTI is trading quietly in a half-dollar range above $63.

USD: The range the Dollar Index carved on August 22, the day Fed Chair Powell spoke at Jackson Hole, remains operative. Only a convincing break of it is important from a technical perspective (~97.55-98.85). Everything else is churn. Today is about the jobs data. Sure, the Fed has two mandates: full employment and price stability. Yet, it is clear that the deterioration of the labor market is the new new thing, not inflation being above target. Isn't this what Powell meant when he suggested that the risk assessment may be changing? The July JOLTS on Wednesday showed that for the first time since April 2021, the number of unemployed outstripped job openings. Just as troubling was the decline in health care job openings in July to the lowest level in five years, and that sector accounted for around 40% of all new jobs in the past three years. Powell was also clear that the supply of workers (via immigration) has fallen at the same time demand has slowed. The unemployment rate is a useful metric of the balance of supply and demand, while the non-farm payroll, which captures the immediate attention of market participants, only captures the demand. The median forecast in Bloomberg's survey is for a 75k rise in non-farm payrolls, though after the soft ADP estimate, some will claim a lower whisper number - whatever that really means. The unemployment rate is expected to rise to 4.3% (from 4.2%). Moreover, next Tuesday, the BLS will announce the preliminary benchmark revisions to the establishment survey, which generates the non-farm payroll estimate. The market is not waiting for next week's CPI, which is expected to see another small rise in the headline rate to fully discount a rate cut at the conclusion of the FOMC meeting on September 17.

Euro: The euro set the session low yesterday in North America, despite ADP reporting weaker-than-expected private sector job creation and softer US rates. It has recovered and is trading near a three-day high in the European morning near $1.1690 despite unexpectedly poor German factory orders. Germany factory orders fell by a whopping 2.9% in July, owing to a collapse of large orders. The fact that the June decline was revised to a 0.2% fall instead of the 1.0% drop initially reported offered slight consolation. Industrial production figures are due Monday, and previously, economists expected a 1.2% gain after the 1.9% slump in June. Recall that after the July US jobs data on August 1, the euro rallied almost two cents and settled slightly below $1.1590. There are almost 2.1 bln euros in options struck at $1.16 that expire today and another 1.5 bln euros there that expire Monday. Also, on Monday, options for 1.12 bln euros at $1.17 expire. France is the center of a maelstrom next week. There are really two components. First is the vote of confidence on the 2026 budget. It stands a snowball's chance in Hades of being approved. President Macron must know this and likely has been considering the next candidate for prime minister. The second component of the French story takes place at the end of the week when Fitch announces its review of the AA- rating it ascribes to France, with a negative outlook. A downgrade is possible, if not likely, given the failure to rein in the budget. It was 5.8% of GDP in 2024, and little improvement is seen this year. Some fear a contagion impact to the periphery and especially Italy.

CNY: The dollar recorded the low for the year against the offshore yuan at the end of last week near CNH7.1160. It recovered to almost CNH7.15 on Tuesday and consolidated in Tuesday's range over the past couple of sessions. The greenback slipped to a four-day low today, slightly above CNH7.13. After lowering the dollar's reference rate for the past several weeks, the PBOC steadied it this week. None of this week's fixes (including today's at CNY7.1064) were below last Friday's (CNY7.1030). It is only the fourth week since mid-April that the dollar's fix did not fall on a weekly basis. Many of China's critics want the yuan to appreciate, but the dispute now seems more over the pace than direction.

JPY: Despite a further softening of US interest rates yesterday, the dollar found better traction against the yen after initially dipping to JPY147.80 to take out Wednesday's low. The greenback set session highs in early North American turnover, after the soft ADP was reported, to almost JPY48.80. It is trading quietly with a softer bias inside yesterday's range. Labor cash earnings rose 4.1% year-over-year in July, well above the June pace of 3.1%, which was the most this year. It was sufficient to lift real earnings to 0.5%, which is also the strongest reading of the year. Nevertheless, while income or wealth is necessary for consumption, it is not sufficient. Household spending (reported in real terms) disappointed. It rose 1.4% year-over-year in July. The median forecast in Bloomberg's survey anticipated a 2.3% increase. The odds of a hike in October were shaved to a little more than 50%. It was around 67% at the end of last week.

GBP: Sterling traded near a four-week low on Wednesday slightly below $1.3335, before recovering to about $1.3460. It did not have the strength yesterday to push above it, and what appeared to be profit-taking pushed it back to nearly $1.3415. It rebounded to around $1.3480 today. UK retail sales (reported in volume terms) rose by 0.6% in July. However, the statistical office acknowledged a seasonal adjustment error that impacted a range of data, including retail sales, but also GDP, prices, and the labor market. The error means that retail sales rose 1.1% in H1 '25, not 1.7% - about a GBP2 bln error. June's initial 0.9% increase in retail sales was revised to 0.3%.

CAD: The greenback rose to a six-day high around CAD1.3845 yesterday to approach the (61.8%) retracement of the decline since Powell spoke at Jackson Hole around CAD1.3850. Between CAD1.3850 and CAD1.3860, more than $1 bln in options expire today. Support is seen in the CAD1.3775 area. In today's softer US dollar environment, the Canadian dollar is the laggard among the G10 currencies. Canada reports August jobs data today. The reaction to the US employment report sets the tone and the broad direction, but in terms of policy outlook for the Bank of Canada, the data could be impactful. The swaps market lifted the chances of a cut later this month after the disappointing Q2 GDP last week. The odds have risen to around 70% from about 55% at the end of last week.

AUD: The Australian dollar continues to coil. It recorded a range of roughly $0.6485-0.6460 on Tuesday and traded inside it on Wednesday (~$0.6500-0.6555). It held above $0.6500 yesterday where options for almost A$1.1 bln expire today, and another set for nearly the same amount struck at $0.6600 that also expire today. It is firm but has held yesterday's high. Australia has a quiet economic diary in the week ahead.

MXN: Mexico's economic diary is light ahead of the weekend, leaving it at the mercy of the dollar's reaction to the employment report. The greenback continues to chop inside Tuesday's range (~MXN18.6225-18.8635). Mexico reports August CPI next Tuesday and July industrial production next Thursday. Barring an upside surprise with the inflation report, and that the core rate does not wander much further above 4.0%, the central bank is likely to ease when it meets the week after the Federal Reserve. The current target rate is 7.75%, and the swaps market sees the terminal rate between 7.0% and 7.25%.

Original Post

data.title: Dividend Power: 2 ETFs That Could Supercharge Your Income Portfolio
data.description:
Introduction

With the rising popularity of covered call funds and new ones seemingly hitting the stock market every week, it's become much easier for investors to generate solid income streams. Moreover, these also eliminate the need to sell call & put options on your individual holdings as the work is done for you.

As someone who's always preferred to collect income from the stock market rather than assets like rental properties to sidestep potential tenant headaches, covered call ETFs are my go-to for building an income stream.

Although many are considered risky due to consistent NAV erosion, which eventually leads to price decay, I believe they deserve their spot inside an income-focused portfolio.

And while I wouldn't necessarily consider these two funds long-term holdings, they could help supercharge your income for other growth-oriented investments.

In this article, I discuss two covered call ETFs income-oriented investors should consider if looking to generate solid streams of passive income.

Why Dividends/Distributions Matter

Depending on who you talk to, some may say that dividends, or distributions (used interchangeably), don't matter and that they are tax inefficient. While this can be true, tell that to the investors currently collecting income and living off it in retirement.

Or better yet, tell that to the investors who currently use dividends to fund their lifestyle, or retire early instead of waiting until the traditional retirement age of 67. If you or your parents were able to generate an income stream of $5,000 or $10,000 from the stock market, would you have worked until your 60's or 70's?

And for those who say that dividends don't matter, the cash that hits your brokerage accounts and pays your bills says otherwise. Below is a chart of dividend contributions to the stock market from 1960 until 2024, and the difference in returns including dividends was more than $5 million.

Below is a chart showing the growth of derivative income over the past decade, which stayed relatively steady until 2021/2022. I don't think it's a coincidence that this is around when the FED hiked interest rates their fastest in history starting in March of 2022.

And although interest rates are projected to come down this year and likely be much lower over the next few years, I think the skyrocketing popularity of covered call ETFs will continue for the foreseeable future. Without further ado, let's get into these two covered call ETFs.

#1 Bitwise COIN Option Income Strategy ETF (ICOI)

First on the list is a new covered call ETF, which debuted just 5 months ago, the Bitwise COIN Option Income Strategy ETF. The fund has an inception date of April 1, 2025, and according to Seeking Alpha, has a distribution rate of 72.02%.

ICOI's primary objective is to provide monthly income, and the fund does this by using a synthetic call strategy selling call & put options on Coinbase Global (COIN). Management uses COIN as a reference asset and sells options between 0% and 15% above the strike price.

ICOI also invests in short-term U.S. treasuries for fixed income and has a total of 13 holdings. The fund is actively managed, which is a positive since crypto assets are typically very dynamic and volatile.

As a result, management uses FLEX options, which means they can adjust expiration dates and strike prices instead of fixed schedules like some competitors. FLEX options also have guaranteed settlements.

ICOI has an expense ratio of 0.98%, meaning roughly it costs you $100 for every $10,000 invested. For comparison, this is higher than other popular, covered-call funds like JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI) & NEOS NASDAQ-100(R) High Income ETF (QQQI), whose expense ratios were more attractive at 0.35% and 0.68%, respectively.

But ICOI's expense ratio is slightly lower than a lot of YieldMax's option income ETFs, whose expense ratios average around 0.99%. A huge risk investing in covered call ETFs is some experience consistent NAV erosion. Although their track record is very short, ICOI's price has declined since inception, while the S&P (SP500) is up closer to 15%.

And going forward, the fund could experience further price and NAV erosion, possibly leading to mediocre returns.

But if you're an income-focused investor, the distributions ICOI pays could be considered worth it. Their latest distribution in August was $5.16 a share. July was their largest so far, with $9.24 paid.

Another good thing about ICOI is that their distributions are considered return of capital, making them more tax efficient and feasible to be held in a taxable account. Below you can see their August distribution was 100% return of capital, meaning taxes are deferred until you decide to sell.

#2 YieldMax SMCI Option Income Strategy ETF (SMCY)

Second on the list is a more popular covered call ETF, YieldMax's SMCI Option Income Strategy ETF. The ETF has a slightly longer track record than ICOI with an inception date of September 11, 2024, so roughly a year.

Similar to Bitwise's option income ETF, SMCY sells synthetic options against Super Micro Computer (SMCI) and holds U.S. treasuries.

Unlike COIN, however, who has performed well over the past year, up nearly 62%, Super Micro Computer is down over 7%.

Since inception, SMCY has consistently declined, down over 70% in the past year, while the S&P is up double digits.

Because of the consistent price decay, SMCY's current distribution yield is a staggering 170.5%, attractive if you're strictly an income investor. Potential underperformance is something investors should be aware of if considering investing in YieldMax ETFs, as many of their funds have underperformed in terms of price.

SMCY has seen the largest price decline over the past year in comparison to its peers, down 71%, while SMCI is down only 7%. And this makes them high-risk investments. But at their current price of $14.64, now may be a good time to buy as the ETF is much more attractive compared to October of last year, when they traded above $55 a share.

And while distributions have declined since inception, SMCY's latest distribution of $1.3081 was still solid, especially considering the price. So, again, if you're looking strictly for income, SMCY may be a good investment at the current price.

According to their website, SMCY's latest distribution was 0% return of capital and considered 100% income, meaning it's taxed at the normal rate. So, unlike Bitwise COIN Option Income Strategy ETF, it may be best to hold SMCY inside a tax-advantaged account like a Roth IRA.

Risks

One risk investors should consider when investing in these two ETFs is their short track records, limiting historical data to analyze. Another risk is the potential for both to experience consistent NAV erosion, leading to underperformance.

High potential for underperformance is something investors should consider when looking to invest in covered call ETFs. And because both don't hold shares directly in Coinbase Global & Super Micro Computer, this caps their upside potential if both stocks experience outperformance.

Conclusion

If you're an income-focused investor looking to generate a solid stream of income, then ICOI and SMCY may be suitable investments for your portfolio.

While there are better covered call ETFs that have experienced less price and NAV decay, both funds have much more attractive prices in comparison to covered call ETFs like JEPI & QQQI.

Moreover, they could help supercharge your income stream when balanced with more growth-focused investments inside your portfolio.

data.title: What Income Investors Need To Know About BITO's Massive Yield
data.description:
The ProShares Bitcoin ETF (NYSEARCA:BITO) currently boasts a 57% trailing 12-month distribution yield, which obviously jumps off the page. Coupled with underlying exposure to Bitcoin (BTC-USD), it can give income investors incredible excitement about the potential for generating massive yield while also delivering phenomenal total returns, given Bitcoin’s track record over the past few years of creating breathtaking wealth for investors.

However, my view is that the fund is structured such that it provides unattractive risk-reward for either generating income or seeking to maximize total returns over time from Bitcoin exposure. Therefore, in this article, I will explain why BITO is not the sustainable yield machine it may appear to be and why I avoid it.

The Allure of BITO’s Yield

BITO’s main strength is obviously the tremendous yield that it has delivered, with its trailing 12-month yield sitting at a mouth-watering 57%. Moreover, the monthly distributions add further appeal to the fund as an income machine for income-focused investors who want exposure to Bitcoin.

However, that income has come at an immense cost, as the chart below illustrates that BITO has lost 53.8% of its value since the fund launched in late 2021. Over the same period, Bitcoin itself has increased in market price by over 79%.

That indicates that not only is the yield taking away from the gains in the underlying Bitcoin, but it is also eroding NAV itself and thereby is not a sustainable source of income over the long term. Moreover, since BITO’s launch, Bitcoin has significantly outperformed BITO in terms of total returns, indicating that its strategy, at the very least, significantly caps upside during strong bull markets for Bitcoin.

Why BITO Is Inferior to Simply Holding Bitcoin

Additionally, if you are really bullish on Bitcoin’s future, BITO appears to be an inferior way to gain exposure to it than simply owning Bitcoin directly or even a spot Bitcoin ETF (IBIT). On top of that, it is important to keep in mind that BITO does not even own Bitcoin, which sort of defeats the purpose of investing in Bitcoin in the first place.

This is because Bitcoin’s main allure is that it is a decentralized investment that cannot be easily linked to its owner and cannot be controlled by governments. It can be transported across borders and exchanged with others through the blockchain without government regulation or interference.

With BITO, you lose all of that and instead are simply putting capital into an actively managed fund that makes bets on Bitcoin futures. This also forces the fund to consistently roll expiring contracts into new ones, which creates frictional costs on the fund. Therefore, the distributions are simply transferring cash through portfolio transactions rather than representing true gains. This explains why BITO’s NAV has declined over time despite Bitcoin itself roaring higher over the same period.

The Binary Nature of Bitcoin’s Future

Perhaps the biggest reason I do not like BITO relative to simply holding underlying Bitcoin is that Bitcoin has such a binary potential future outcome. By that, I mean that buying Bitcoin is essentially making a bet that its network will be widely adopted by mainstream institutions and society. If it successfully achieves this status, it will generate incredibly high returns from here. If it fails, it will result in disastrous losses for investors.

If Bitcoin remains a fringe medium of exchange useful only in black-market transactions or in niche companies, it will likely eventually lose its appeal to investors and collapse to a very low value, in which case BITO will also crash in value. However, if the likes of Amazon (AMZN), Walmart (WMT), and Costco (COST) begin to accept it as a medium of exchange, and U.S. regulators allow it to challenge the U.S. dollar (UUP) in the domestic and global economy, Bitcoin will likely soar in value, in which case, BITO will likely also continue to deliver attractive returns. Not only would that increase Bitcoin's value simply due to consumer demand for the cryptocurrency increasing, but it would also help Bitcoin stabilize at a much higher level and become a valid store of value, potentially prompting central banks and institutions to accumulate it as a reserve asset similar to gold (GLD).

This binary nature also explains the logic behind Michael Saylor’s increasingly leveraged bet on Bitcoin at MicroStrategy (MSTR). He knows that if Bitcoin goes to zero, leverage will not matter—he will go broke either way. But if Bitcoin fulfills its potential, the more leverage he employs, the richer he becomes. In that sense, pursuing a strategy like BITO, which causes investors to lag in many bullish scenarios for Bitcoin, while not offering meaningful downside protection if Bitcoin collapses, makes little sense.

Investor Takeaway

If you are an income investor looking to generate dependable income, Bitcoin-related securities are not the place to be invested at this stage in Bitcoin's investment cycle. Instead, it should be, at most, a small speculative piece of your portfolio. I myself maintain a very small speculative position in Bitcoin, but I do not view it as a source of income. For generating yield, there are far better opportunities in high-quality bonds (BND), preferreds (PFFA), and dividend stocks (SCHD). Moreover, if I want to hedge against fiat currency debasement and the dollar losing global reserve currency status, I can generate significant income from dividend-paying gold mining stocks (GDX), selling covered calls against gold and silver ETFs, investing in funds that do so for me (GLDI)(SLVO), or even investing in gold and silver bullion leases.

As a result, I am steering clear of BITO in favor of a small speculative Bitcoin position and am then investing the bulk of my portfolio into high-quality dividend-paying stocks, preferreds, bonds, and income-generating precious metals investments in my portfolio at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Diamond Hill Short Duration Securitized Bond Strategy Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

The Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index returned +1.21% in Q2, adding to the strong performance in Q1 (+2.78%) and bringing year-to-date performance for the index to +4.02%. The 2025 year-to-date performance represents the best six-month start to a calendar year since 2020’s return of +6.14%.

The story of the second quarter was all about the journey and not the destination, as the markets were roiled by Liberation Day and higher-than-expected tariffs across the board. The S&P 500 posted its fifth-largest two-day decline since World War II, but ended the quarter at a record high as tariff delays and alterations mitigated most of the damage wrought early in the quarter. The fixed income markets followed a similar path, dropping precipitously in the early days of April before rallying in the latter part of the quarter.

Q2 was one of the most chaotic and unsettling quarters in recent memory as the market dealt with the fallout from Liberation Day, oil price volatility driven by the short-lived Israel-Iran conflict and US fiscal concerns. The combination of the passage of the Big Beautiful Bill, as well as the final nail in the AAA US credit quality coffin, with Moody’s finally joining S&P and Fitch in downgrading US debt to AA+, led to a move higher on the longer end of the Treasury curve. Adding to the chaos of the quarter was the ongoing name-calling and demand for the resignation of Jerome Powell, making Q2 one for the ages. But despite the tumultuous journey, the result was relatively strong performance across the board.

Exhibit 1 — Cumulative Performance Q2 2025 (%)

Source: Bloomberg. IG Corp – Investment grade corporates. Securitized – Bloomberg US Securitized Index. Treasury – 10Y US Treasury Yield. The MOVE Index tracks volatility in the US Treasury market in order to provide insight into the fixed income markets. It utilizes options-pricing models based on a weighted average of option probabilities to reflect expectations for future volatility in fixed income markets.

It was a challenging start to the quarter as Liberation Day delivered significantly higher and broader tariffs than the market had been expecting. A flat across-the-board 10% tariff for all countries, with higher rates for specific countries based on the overall trade relationship. As we all know, markets dislike uncertainty, and this was reflected in the performance across all risk assets with investors pricing in a global downturn. The Treasury market was no different, with the yield on the 10-year Treasury and 30-year Treasury jumping 50 basis points (bps) and 46 bps, respectively, during the week following Liberation Day. This disruption led to the 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs, triggering a massive rally. As the quarter progressed, markets fell prey to every news flash and announcement, ranging from the back-and-forth tariff escalation between China and the US to rulings from the US Court of International Trade.

Amid all the volatility tied to the global trade situation, Moody’s finally joined S&P (August 2011) and Fitch (August 2023) in downgrading US government debt from AAA to AA+/Aa1. Moody’s cited the government’s inability to agree on measures to reverse the trend of large fiscal deficits and ever-increasing interest costs. The announcement from Moody’s felt like a non-event in the markets, almost as if the broad response was, “What took so long for you to catch up with the rest of the world?”. Compounding the concerns about the fiscal situation was the passage of Trump’s signature bill that extends tax cuts, launches a new child savings account and increases spending on immigration reform and military spending. However, the reductions don’t meet or exceed the increase in spending, resulting in additional debt being added to the already substantial US budget deficit. According to the Congressional Budget Office, the impact of the bill’s passage would be an increase in the deficit of $1.7 trillion over the next five years and an increase of $2.4 trillion over the next ten years.

In the face of the turmoil besetting the market throughout the quarter, economic data remained resilient. Various economic data points continued to indicate that the economy was expanding and on somewhat solid ground. The labor market generated an average of 154,000 jobs per month, including revisions, while the unemployment rate remained at 4.2% in April and May before slightly decreasing to 4.1%. While concerns persist regarding the impact of tariffs on inflation, core inflation remained at 2.8% throughout the quarter, following a 3.1% finish in the first quarter. The Federal Reserve’s two main responsibilities, price stability and employment, remained in manageable territory and kept the Fed on the sidelines during its two meetings during the quarter.

While the Federal Reserve has remained on the sidelines, they have not stayed out of the administration’s crosshairs. Continued calls from President Trump for the resignation of Jerome Powell have fueled the financial news networks, but do not seem to have rattled the Chair. The prospect of the Shadow Fed Chair has been discussed, which essentially would be the nomination or designation of Powell’s successor before his term expires (May 2026). However, even the prospect of a Shadow Fed Chair does not seem sufficient to derail the Fed’s current approach of patience and data dependency. Monetary policy is determined by the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC), not the Fed Chair themself. Powell is one of twelve voting members on the FOMC, and possible replacements would likely maintain the institution’s autonomy. While any Shadow Fed Chair could create some uncertainty risk in the market, the power still resides with the FOMC.

Exhibit 2 — Powell Has Been a Consensus Builder (%)

Source: Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis. Data represents the percentage of FOMC voting members who voted in favor of monetary policy directives set by the Presiding Chair of the FOMC.

Treasury Update

While the Treasury market delivered a positive performance during the quarter (+0.85% for the Bloomberg US Treasury Index), it lagged both the corporate and securitized sectors, pulling the overall performance of the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index lower. The main culprit for the drop in performance was the twisting of the curve as the shorter end yield moved lower while the longer end pushed higher. The 2-year Treasury yield dropped from 3.89% to 3.72% during the quarter, while the 30- year Treasury yield climbed from 4.59% to 4.78% as concerns about the fiscal situation intensified. The impact of the yield shift is evident in the performance of the Treasury Bellwether securities: the 2-year Treasury Bellwether returned +1.12% and the 30-year Treasury Bellwether returned -2.08% in Q2.

The December Fed funds futures moved nearly in lockstep with the 2-year Treasury, shifting throughout the month as incoming economic data and trade gyrations fueled the moves through the quarter. The volatility during the quarter resulted in significant action across the curve, despite the fact that quarter-over-quarter movements in rates were somewhat muted. Consider that the 2-year Treasury finished 17 bps lower during the quarter but ranged from as low as 3.60% to a peak of 4.05%, a range of 45 bps. The longer end of the curve experienced even greater ranges, with the 10-year yield moving higher by only one basis point but covering a range of roughly 61 bps during the quarter. The 30-year Treasury yield rose 19 bps from the end of March to the end of June but fluctuated between 4.41% and 5.09% before settling at 4.78% at quarter-end.

Exhibit 3 — Cumulative Implied Rate Change Through Year-End (%)

Source: Bloomberg.

Corporate Update

Much like the rest of the market, the investment-grade corporate sector experienced significant fluctuations throughout the quarter but ended June with spread levels tighter than where they began. Spread is the additional yield over comparable duration Treasuries that compensates investors for the additional credit risk. Investment-grade corporate spreads, as measured by the Bloomberg US Corporate Bond Index, traded in a range from 118.5 bps to 83.2 bps, which is where the index finished the quarter. The spread level for the index reached 118.5 bps in the days following Liberation Day, as perceived risk increased with the uncertainty of the impact of tariffs on the corporate market.

The yield on the corporate index followed a similar path, jumping roughly +0.40% from the beginning of below 5.00% by the end of June. Despite the volatility during the quarter, investors continue to clamor for the sector, as the overall yield it provides remains attractive. However, spread levels in the corporate market remain tight, both historically and relative to other areas of the market.

Exhibit 4 — Investment Grade Corporate Spreads (bps)

Source: Bloomberg. Bloomberg US Corporate Bond Index vs Treasury Yield.

Securitized Update

Fluctuations in the rates market led to a divergence within the mortgage market, as better-structured collateralized mortgage obligations (CMOs) held up much better than plain-vanilla mortgage passthrough securities. CMOs, as measured by the ICE BofA CMO Index, returned +1.48% while passthroughs, as measured by the Bloomberg US RMBS Index, returned +1.14%. Elsewhere within the securitized market, non-agency commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) continued to deliver decent performance (+2.00% during the quarter) while shorter-duration asset-backed securities (ABS) (+1.38%) continued the slow and steady pace of shorter-duration securities with attractive spread levels.

Despite the volatility in the corporate sector during the quarter, spreads within that sector remain tight from a historical standpoint, and better-yielding and structured securitized segments of the market continue to offer better relative value for managers willing to dig into the details of the structures and underlying collateral. Issuance in the securitized market remains strong, despite the slowdown experienced in mid-April. While off the pace from the record levels of 2024, 2025 is issuing at a run rate that would place production near the record level and comfortably in second position historically.

Consumer-related structured product remittance reports indicate a stabilizing consumer, with 30-day delinquencies for subprime and prime auto loans increasing by 0.1% to 14.6% and 1.8%, respectively, while credit card and retail card delinquencies decrease by 0.2% (to 1.3%) and 0.3% (to 3.2%), respectively. Delinquencies in consumer personal loans decreased in Traditional loans (-0.4% to 5.8%) and remained flat in Marketplace Lending at 4.5%. Over the last twelve months, Subprime Auto has increased by 0.6% and Retail Card has increased by 0.4% while Marketplace Lending has decreased by 0.7% with Prime Auto, Traditional Consumer Loans and Credit Card fairly stable.

Portfolio Positioning and Performance

It is important to note our portfolio works to provide yield for investors while focusing on the shorter end of fixed income markets. We believe there are opportunities to add incremental yield over the benchmark by investing in structured products across the quality spectrum. The portfolio strives to maintain an average credit quality rating of A/BBB while capitalizing on mispriced opportunities in unrated securities and allocating to below-investment-grade securities.

As of the end of the second quarter, the portfolio had a yield-to-worst (YTW) of 6.55% with an effective duration of 1.29 years, compared to a YTW of 6.45% and an effective duration of 1.33 years at the end of Q1. The decrease in yield is reflective of the shift lower in the yield curve as well as overall spread tightening since the end of the first quarter, despite the volatility early in Q2.

The portfolio’s allocation to the corporate and Treasury sectors detracted from its relative performance during the quarter, as these sectors were unable to keep pace with the performance of the securitized allocation, although both delivered positive returns. Spreads on the shorter end of the corporate curve finished the quarter at compressed levels and remain tight relative to various areas of the securitized sector.

Performance in the securitized allocation was driven by continued strength in the non-agency residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS) and CMBS sectors, both of which continue to benefit from investors’ search for yield in markets that continue to recover from prior years’ challenges.

Within non-agency CMBS, areas such as Single Asset Single Borrower (SASB) securities and commercial real estate collateralized loan obligations (CRE CLOs) led the way, from office to industrial. While the ABS allocation was fueled by strong performance in the Equipment and Unsecured Consumer allocations. With interest rates on a rollercoaster during the quarter, well-structured CMOs held up well but did not keep pace with stronger areas of the securitized market.

The duration of the portfolio remains positioned below the midpoint of the targeted duration of 1 to 2.

We continue to search for opportunities in the marketplace while maintaining an attractive yield relative to the benchmark.

Bonds rated AAA, AA, A and BBB are considered investment grade.

ICE BAML MOVE Index is a measure of US interest rate volatility that tracks the movement in US Treasury yield volatility implied by current prices of one-month over-the-counter options on 2-year, 5-year, 10-year and 30-year Treasurys.
