data.title: Oracle: Why I Loaded Up Before Earnings And What I'm Looking Out For
data.description:
Oracle (ORCL) provides general-purpose cloud infrastructure, but often overshadowed by larger cloud providers, as their market share of storage as a service is only at 2%-3%, which shows that competition is high with major players like Google (GOOGL)(GOOG) Cloud, AWS (AMZN) and Microsoft Azure (MSFT).

Oracle is differentiated by having deep roots in enterprise databases, positioning itself to not just be a general cloud provider but one that is a specialist in providing data as well as enterprise resource planning [ERP] which companies use to manage their whole systems, like payroll and inventory. Rather than compete with these hyperscalers, Oracle partners with them so customers can link their enterprise data to Oracle's Cloud Infrastructure [OCI] so they can use Oracle's software. This can include major banks and hospitals that run their whole database on Oracle, as Oracle provides security, updates, and all-day technical support.

They are indispensable as the switching costs would be high to move databases, as Oracle continues to grow its cloud services and license support revenue fast with 77% YoY from FY24 to FY25. However, the high debt is a huge concern if there is any slowdown in revenue growth/net income with valuations to closely monitor, with growth stocks having high valuations, which is always a concern. Hence, I'm rating Oracle a 'Buy'.

Thesis

The AI boom hasn't shown any signs of slowing down yet, with Oracle's competitors/partners still spending big on capex in the most recent quarter. Google increased capex by $10B to $85B with Amazon spending $31.4B in a single quarter on capex, which shows that the AI boom isn't slowing down yet. Oracle continues to benefit as enterprises shift to the cloud and use Oracle's services, which leads to a larger moat for Oracle, as Oracle is harder to displace the more data it has on its database.

Oracle offers software as a service (SaaS) whose demand is growing as more and more enterprises shift to the cloud and require their SaaS, which includes ERP and human capital management [HCM]. With no signs of cloud adoption slowing down yet, with AWS growing revenue 17% YoY, Google Cloud 39% YoY and Azure 32% YoY, Oracle is positioned to provide SaaS to enterprises. Their main growth driver, infrastructure as a service (IaaS), provides compute, storage, and networking in the cloud. This database hosts its database and SaaS, whose growth is accelerating. This is seen with their remaining performance obligation [RPO] still being high, with only 33% of their total RPO recognized as revenue in the next 12 months.

However, there are key metrics to look out for in the earnings call and guidance. With high valuations and premiums, investors expect high-growth rates, which are only guided up 16% YoY for FY26. Other metrics like capex and margins also have to be closely monitored, as these cloud firms are in a prisoner's dilemma, which leads to increased capex when one firm increases capex, as they don't wanna fall behind. With AWS posting poor growth YoY relative to Google and Microsoft and lower margins due to capex leading to a huge dip post-earnings, their metrics have to be able to back up their valuation.

Oracle's Growth Catalysts

Oracle's main growth driver is cloud services and license support, with an impressive growth of 77% YoY. From the recent earnings call, it is noted that this revenue is entirely subscription-based, so recurring revenues show that Oracle is positioned for growth, as unless enterprises cancel their subscription and move on to someone else (unlikely), Oracle's revenue is unlikely to get displaced.

Cloud Services Revenue Growth Rate YoY
IaaS/Cloud Infrastructure $10.2B 51%
SaaS $14.3B 10%

Out of the $44B revenue and $24.5B in cloud services revenue, IaaS stood at $10.2B, SaaS stood at $14.3B, and license support fell YoY from $19.6B to $19.5B. However, this is not concerning, as license support is a one-time license fee and a recurring net license fee, which is set at 22% of the net license fee. With a shift to the cloud seen with the cloud's growth rate, this shows that the industry is shifting towards cloud-based solutions as more and more enterprises adopt Oracle.

Oracle is business-oriented, as they appeal to enterprises that need their software services as well as cloud. Their Cloud is optimized for their own SaaS and their IaaS, as they are able to appeal to enterprises for IaaS as it is cheaper than its peers. From Oracle, it is noted that cloud infrastructure is noted to be 57% cheaper than AWS and Azure, while being 51% cheaper than Google Cloud. They offer cheaper cloud services across regions, but Oracle provides its services to enterprises, as they can manage their software like ERP and HCM easily on Oracle's Cloud. This is how Oracle can appeal to others to use their cloud services over major hyperscalers like AWS, which has more services, but rather be business-focused so it can appeal to enterprises.

A key advantage of appealing to enterprises is once they start using Oracle, they get into their ecosystem of SaaS and IaaS, so they continue to store more and more data on their cloud rather than AWS, which may charge more. With so much data on their system, Oracle has a wide moat over others with high switching costs, as other cloud services are more expensive as well and still need to use Oracle's software services anyway, so the shift of data from one cloud provider to another is not rational for businesses and unlikely to happen. With Oracle's security and recovery times having no data loss except for complete storage failures, which only have potential data loss of only up to 5 minutes, for enterprises there is no appeal to switch to other cloud providers, as Oracle provides a perfectly good, efficient service. Oracle is also certified for many of its different services, which shows that it is a secure and valid website based on its attestations.

SaaS did only grow 10% YoY, which is not super impressive, but Oracle grows with IaaS as SaaS is just a yearly subscription or amount per user, whilst their cloud and storage revenue is generated based off the amount of cloud storage that the enterprise needs, which is why IaaS grows at a more impressive rate. So all enterprises pay the flat annual fee or amount per user to access the software, but all enterprises need different amounts of storage, as the amount of data stored per employee will be different depending on the enterprise itself.

Also mentioned in the earnings call:

We are currently live in 23 cloud regions with database at cloud services and have another 47 planned.

This shows the continued push towards expansion of data centers as well as the Stargate project leading to building massive data centers alongside OpenAI, with cloud regions of up to 70. With huge expansion and improved infrastructure, their AI capabilities can be used more effectively and generate more revenue and a higher RPO for Oracle. There are estimates that Stargate could generate $30B - $60B annual revenue for Oracle, which is a huge growth catalyst. If Oracle becomes the norm for enterprises as their cloud continues to grow at large paces, Oracle's revenue could easily grow to over $100B annually, with Stargate as the guided revenue for FY26 from the earnings call being $67B.

With Europe/Middle East/Africa and APAC only growing at only 7.6% and 3.3%, this shows that there is huge room to grow in these regions if Oracle gets more and more widely adopted. With their plans of increasing live cloud regions by over 200%, is very bullish for Oracle, as the total addressable market increases as more and more enterprises are able to access Oracle.

Pre-Earnings Focus

Metrics like capex, operating margin, revenue growth, and RPO are key metrics I'm looking out for.

With huge macroeconomic uncertainties, estimates for the recent quarter have been slightly lower as firms are expected to take losses and larger costs due to the US-China headwinds. This led to 81% of S&P 500 companies beating sales and EPS (top and bottom line) estimates, with Information Technology being the most notable. Due to lower estimates, more firms than not have been beating the top and bottom line, with investors/analysts heavily looking out for guidance, so a top and bottom line beat should be expected, with Amazon, Google, and Microsoft beating the top and bottom line with ease, with beats on revenue being at least $2B. I expect a comfortable beat for Oracle, as cloud demand hasn't shown any sign of slowing down, with 17%, 39%, and 32% YoY revenue growth from AWS, Google Cloud, and Azure, respectively.

Focusing on capex, it is notable that hyperscalers are increasing capex, with Google's increased capex to $85B, up $10B even in a time of macroeconomic uncertainty, while Amazon has an annualized capex rate of $118B, which is a huge amount since Oracle's FY25 revenue was only $57.4B

Due to being in a prisoner's dilemma, Oracle is also spending huge capex in the cloud as they continue to expand, as noted above, increasing regions from 23 to 70, over 200%.

This would affect their operating margin, though operating margin GAAP did increase from 29% to 31% from FY24 to FY25. The gap between non-GAAP and GAAP margins, where non-GAAP margins don't measure one-off capexes, showed a disparity of 42%, as the GAAP margin is 31% to non-GAAP's 44%.

Note: This is Amazon's balance sheet, with the far left being Q1 2024, going by quarter to Q2 2025 on the far right

With the most recent quarter's operating margin at 32%, maintaining it at 32% would be a huge bullish sign for Oracle, as other cloud providers like AWS had their operating margin fall from 39.5% down to 32.9%, which is a huge contraction. The operating margin here for AWS would stabilize back as it includes a one-off capex charge, but if Oracle is able to increase capex and maintain a GAAP operating margin near the 32% mark, it would be very bullish for Oracle. Investors will expect the non-GAAP mark to be also near 44%, and it should be no surprise with both FY24 and FY25 at 44% non-GAAP operating margins.

Mentioned in their earnings call:

So for fiscal year 2026, I expect that total cloud revenue will grow over 40% in constant currency

I expect total revenue will be at least $67 billion, up 16% in constant currency and up more than $1 billion from our prior guidance.

(Q1) Total revenues are expected to grow from 11% to 13% in constant currency are expected to grow from 12% to 14% in U.S. dollars. Total cloud revenue is expected to grow from 26% to 30% in constant currency and U.S. dollars.

Revenue growth will be key, as Amazon fell as much as 10% post-earnings as its AWS 17% growth lagged Google's and Azure's at 39% and 32%. Oracle's cloud is smaller than hyperscalers, so if they guide for 28% (midpoint of 26% and 30%) revenue growth, investors want to see 28% growth with high premiums on Oracle. If they don't achieve the 28% mark and don't have a good reason, like heavy weighting towards next quarter/2nd half of a higher guidance for next quarter, this will create huge negative sentiment for Oracle, as the result of good/poor cloud results is clear with the huge dip directly post-earnings for Amazon and a huge rise directly post-earnings for Microsoft.

With minimal tariff impact mentioned in the earnings call:

Sure. Tariffs have no impact really at all to play in this.

Oracle is to beat top and bottom line estimates and achieve expected cloud growth as the base case, as anything below that is bearish, as they noted they aren't affected by macroeconomic headwinds, which many other firms are. Beating estimates is simply the bottom line, with a huge focus on guidance, as any increase during times of uncertainty is very bullish for the stock.

Also mentioned in their earnings call:

Our remaining performance obligations now stand at $138 billion, up $8 billion from last quarter and up 41% from last year, and yet the best is still to come

Further, our cloud RPO grew 56% on top of the 80% growth last year and now represents nearly 80% of total RPO and approximately 33% of total RPO is expected to be recognized as revenue over the next 12 months.

RPO is likely to grow more than 100% in fiscal '26

This continues to show that demand hasn't slowed down for Oracle, as their RPO continues to increase with expectations of a 100% growth in RPO in FY26. Their revenue growth is accelerating, but I have concerns over actual revenue conversion with YoY revenue growth of only 16%. 16% growth isn't bad, but with cloud revenues accelerating for hyperscalers and Oracle, such a huge backlog means that Oracle needs to execute perfectly to impress investors, as $138B in RPO at the end of FY25, which is expected to grow at 100%, and a market cap of only $630B are very impressive.

If their RPO continues to grow at the pace of 100% in FY26 and is assured by management, this is very bullish for Oracle as their backlog is increasing. So even though converting RPO to revenue might be slow, RPO as an indicator of future revenue is a key metric to look out for, especially since they are on track to grow at the 100% mark for FY26, which was assured by management last earnings call. Any assurances above/below the 100% mark would be bullish/bearish for the stock.

If capex increases and operating margin is stable, alongside solid revenue and RPO numbers, this could be a very bullish quarter for Oracle, as the gains after post-earnings have been wiped out (post-earnings to $215 per share, now at $223 a share after 3 months). The S&P 500 gained around 9% from Oracle's earnings to today, which suggests that the post-earnings gain (after the initial rise) has been wiped out, falling from the 52-week high of $260.87 a share.

Risks

Cloud providers are in a prisoner's dilemma, as when one increases their capex, the other has to follow to keep up so they have the most advanced AI infrastructure to maintain high revenue growth and margins, which is what investors pay a premium for. This is seen with AWS showing signs of slow growth and a selloff, which leads to higher capex from Amazon, which pressures other hyperscalers to follow suit. This will continue to fuel the AI boom with more demand for cloud infrastructure, but this affects cash flow and net income with capex continuing to grow without any limits. Oracle doesn't have the cash and revenue like these established hyperscalers, so capex will heavily affect Oracle more than its competitors. This has led to Oracle having $108.95B in debt and $11.2B in cash, which creates a problem for Oracle if capex continues to rise as Oracle follows. Any signs of slowing down could hence affect Oracle quite negatively with their existing debt, so they have to continue to keep up with their RPO and growth to ensure investors that this debt isn't a problem.

Valuation

This is Oracle's Valuation Metrics:

Across the board, Oracle is overvalued compared to the sector median as well as historical averages, as any correction in valuation or the sector could lead to huge downturns for Oracle as investors put a larger premium on Oracle. However, I argue that the valuation numbers are quite distorted due to concentration. ARM has a very high valuation, and I argue it could be contributed to by the fact that SoftBank owns 90% of ARM so the shares that are actually in float are quite low. This can be reflected in Oracle, as Mr. Ellison owns around 40% of Oracle, which the recent earnings sent him to being the 2nd richest man in the world.

Oracle has a float of 1.64B shares with 2.81B outstanding shares, which suggests that it could be more concentrated by around the 41% level. ARM is nearly 90% concentrated and has a P/E TTM of ~200, which is nearly 4x of what Oracle's P/E is. With the last sale by Mr. Ellison in mid-2024 of barely 0.1% of his shares held after the transaction, Oracle's valuation should be higher by around 40% than what it would be because of a higher concentration.

With this also mentioned in their recent earnings call:

And over the last 10 years, we've reduced shares outstanding by more than 1/3 at an average share price of just over $54.

This shows their commitment to investor return as outstanding has shrunk by 33% as well at a price level that is nowhere near. I argue that Oracle's valuations seem inflated to an extent by its concentration, as the amount of shares of Oracle that can actually be traded is way lower than its outstanding shares. Compared to Microsoft, they have a float of 7.42B shares with 7.43 outstanding shares, showing that the concentration compared to Oracle isn't nearly as high.

Comparing P/E and P/S with its hyperscaler peers:

On both P/E and P/S basis, Oracle does seem overvalued, especially on P/E, as getting close to MSFT's P/E would suggest at least a 20% downturn. However, if inclusive of the idea that the stock is at a higher premium due to its concentration, the overvaluation seems justified. On a P/S basis where the one-time capex is removed from a GAAP basis, Oracle seems a bit less overvalued compared to its peers. Amazon's AWS is only 18% of its revenue, with Google Cloud only at 14%, which suggests that Oracle's valuation isn't too out of this world, as Microsoft Cloud stood at ~60% of revenue.

With cloud services and license support at 74% of revenue in its most recent quarter, Oracle's valuation on a P/S could be seen as undervalued compared to Microsoft, as the growth rate of Cloud is higher than Microsoft, with Azure's recent quarter having 32% with total cloud revenue expected to grow 40% in the next fiscal year for Oracle. Including the concentration idea, Oracle could be seen as undervalued, as its premiums would fall if Mr. Ellison were to start diluting. If Mr. Ellison were to start diluting the stock, would fall alongside valuations, which I argue is seen today with Bezos dumping Amazon stocks, which leads it to lower valuations to historical averages. Since Mr. Ellison has shown no interest in dumping yet, I argue that the valuations of Oracle are slightly inflated due to a higher concentration of shares.

Conclusion

I've recently restarted my position in Oracle, as I previously closed my position post-earnings, as my ideas and stance were more convicted towards other shares. With a dip to now around the $220s markdown from a peak of $260, could be a huge opportunity, as a bullish quarter could lead to a breakout to its 52 week high. With most hyperscalers reporting good earnings with no misses, I believe such positive sentiment can also be reflected in Oracle before the earnings. If the stock dips after earnings, I would further add to my position, as the future of Oracle dominating cloud among enterprises with huge expansion plans is only just starting with plans like Stargate. Unless the earnings significantly disappoint, I have a positive/bullish stance on Oracle pre-earnings.

data.title: Visa: Strong, Steady Growth Continues Despite New Tech Challenges
data.description:
No Signs Of Slowing

I rarely write about Visa (V) (VISA:CA) on Seeking Alpha even though it is my second-largest holding and I have owned it starting with the 2008 IPO. The company has been a steady grower along with growth in global payment volumes and adoption of electronic payment methods. That is why in my last article in December 2023, I rated Visa "Still a Buy At All-Time Highs." Since then, the stock has returned just under the 40% total return of the S&P 500 (SP500).

Company performance justifies the stock performance with stable revenue growth around 10%. (Data through 2024 from Seeking Alpha Financials page, 2025 and 2026 estimates from Seeking Alpha Earnings page)

Visa has continued to control its costs well, with operating costs growing about as fast as sales. Net income has grown in the low teens percentages, faster than sales, as the company has been earning positive net interest income thanks to its high cash holdings. Note the dip in 2025 GAAP results includes an unusually high legal provision in the long-running interchange fee case, which should be nearing a conclusion.

EPS growth has been in the mid-teens as Visa has been retiring about 2.5% of the share count each year through buybacks.

After almost 3 years of steady growth, it is a good time to look into whether the drivers of this growth are still present. In Visa's case, these drivers include not just the growing market for payments that is the company's core business, but also value-added services. These service help Visa's bank and merchant partners optimize their performance, differentiate their offerings and create better experiences for their customers. It is also a good time to look at new threats to Visa's business model, such as the growing interest in stablecoins and the degree to which they can disrupt the intermediary service provided by Visa's network.

Growth in global payments volume remains strong with much runway left in many countries for the movement from cash to electronic payment. Visa is also moving into new payment channels beyond the traditional merchant-to-customer transaction. Beyond payments, Visa's value-added services facilitate new card issuance and acceptance, provide security and fraud protection, and provide insights to partners through data analytics. It is these services that provide a premium experience to users beyond the basic payment capabilities crypto and stablecoins offer. This should allow Visa to continue growing sales at the historical 10% per year pace.

Although Visa stock is richly valued based on earnings, it remains cheaper than Mastercard (MA) while its larger size and better balance sheet give it greater capacity to acquire fintechs to keep up with the rapidly changing technical environment. While owning Visa now requires paying up for quality, I continue to rate the stock a Buy.

Growth Drivers

McKinsey & Company's Global Payments Report shows that payments revenue increased at a rate of 7% annually over the last decade, although growth is forecasted to slow to 5% over the next 5 years.

Visa managed to grow revenues faster than this global average over the last decade. The company should continue to grow faster than the industry as it focuses on key activities identified in the report to capture share.

We see three main areas where operators and disrupters will want to invest to capture their share of market growth: instant payments offerings in margin-rich segments where they have the ability to succeed; improvements to anti–money laundering [AML] and fraud prevention systems; and infrastructure and systems that can operate reliably at high volumes.

Recall that Visa's core business is enabling consumer-to-business transactions by operating the network between the consumer's and the merchant's banks. The company does this reliably at high volumes, with 65.4 billion transactions processed in Q3, or around 8.3 per second.

Beyond this model, Visa is increasingly participating in the "instant payments" market through its Visa Direct business. Use cases for Visa Direct include instant payments for gig workers and freelancers, employee and merchant cash advances against future paychecks and credit settlements, insurance claims, refunds, and cross-border remittances. In Q3 2025, Visa Direct transactions grew 25% to 3.3 billion transactions in the quarter.

Value-added services was also a strong grower in Q3, up 26% and earning $2.8 billion in the quarter. These go beyond the fraud protection outlined in the McKinsey report into other services, including issuer and merchant acceptance solutions and data analytics. These services have been enabled by Visa's many acquisitions of fintech companies in recent years. Issuer solutions include Pismo, which provides issuing banks a software platform to quickly roll out new products (cards, deposit accounts, loans). Acceptance solutions include Cybersource, which helps sellers integrate the payment process with e-commerce platforms. Visa enhanced its fraud protection through the acquisition of Featurespace, which uses AI for fraud prevention and risk scoring. Finally, Visa offers a consulting and analytics service that helps its clients analyze transaction data to improve marketing and provides IT support such as financial app development.

But What About Stablecoins?

Visa shares endured a nearly 10% pullback in June as two news items came out. First, Amazon (AMZN) and Walmart (WMT) discussed looking into issuing stablecoins for their retail businesses. Next, Congress passed the GENIUS Act, expanding and regulating stablecoin issuance in the US. The stock regained about half of this pullback prior to the Q3 earnings release. Stablecoins are a form of cryptocurrency, operating on a blockchain, but generally pegged to a fiat currency like the US dollar or Euro, and issuers are required to maintain reserves of cash or cash equivalents backing the stablecoins. The main advantage is the negligible cost of transferring funds, especially compared to international remittance services and credit cards, though not as big of an advantage over debit cards relative to the size of most transactions.

A key disadvantage, at least initially, is the acceptance and convertibility of the stablecoins into and out of fiat currency, which would come at some cost beyond that listed above. Card platforms like Visa are well suited to help with this by allowing transactions in stablecoins just like any other currency and providing treasury services to enable fast convertibility. While there would still be some added cost for this, it would still be a cheap alternative to traditional international remittance services, more along the lines of a fiat currency remittance using Visa Direct.

Networks like Visa also provide security and fraud protection, and cards on the network can offer reward programs for consumers. Direct stablecoin payments do none of this, making the card ecosystem sticky for consumers to abandon. While stablecoins could make a dent in remittance payments and business to business cross border transactions, most analysts do not see them as an existential threat to the existing payment providers.

Valuation And Peer Comparison

Consensus EPS for Visa in 2025 is $11.43 (non-GAAP), growing 12.2% to $12.83 in 2026. That is a P/E of 30.6 and a PEG ratio of 2.5. Mastercard uses a calendar fiscal year, in contrast with the October-September fiscal year used by Visa. Mastercard analyst consensus is for 2025 EPS of $16.32, followed by 16.2% growth to $18.96 in 2026. That results in a P/E of 36.4 and a PEG ratio of 2.25. Valuations of both stocks have increased since my article in 2023, but are not near historic highs. Visa remains cheaper than Mastercard.

Visa has a slightly better dividend yield than Mastercard, but both are low yielders, with Visa's 0.67% yield compared to Mastercard's 0.51%. The last dividend raises were 13% for Visa and 15% for Mastercard, but these are nearly a year old. Visa should announce their next raise in October and Mastercard in December. Since my 2023 article, Visa has gotten slightly more aggressive on buybacks than Mastercard, repurchasing 2.4% to 2.7% of outstanding shares annually the last few years. Mastercard has been more variable, between 1.1% and 2.5%. Visa, therefore, has a slight edge in total capital return despite its many small fintech acquisitions.

While net debt has come up for both companies in the last 3 years, it is still quite low. Despite being a larger company by both market cap and book equity, Visa has less net debt due to its cash balance being almost twice that of Mastercard. This continues to give Visa the edge as a fintech acquirer and as an earner of net interest income in the current higher for longer interest rate environment.

$bn V MA
Market Cap $ 676.19 $ 538.48
Book Equity $ 37.79 $ 7.85
Cash $ 19.18 $ 9.37
Net Debt $ 5.96 $ 9.60
ND/E 0.16 1.22

Conclusion

Visa remains a strong, steady grower as it has been since becoming public, thanks to the secular trend of payments moving from cash to electronic. Visa has supplemented this growth in the last couple of years by acquiring small fintech companies and incorporating them into its value-added services to become more than a payment platform. This technical expertise should allow Visa to continue growing and staying competitive despite threats from competing technology like stablecoins.

Both Visa and Mastercard have high valuations, and both valuations have increased since my last article in 2023. Still, I rate Visa a buy because its near duopoly status in most of the world gives it a wide moat, and its technical capabilities enable it to incorporate, rather than be displaced by, new technologies such as stablecoins. Visa continues to have an advantage over Mastercard because of its size and low net debt. This makes it easier for Visa to buy and develop emerging financial technologies to maintain competitiveness. While I'm not adding to my already large position in Visa shares (except to reinvest dividends), I still think the stock is worth buying at current levels around $350 for the long term.

data.title: Global Commercial Real Estate Outlook: Autumn 2025
data.description:
By Mike Bessell, Managing Director, Investment Strategist, Europe

Examining recent events for potential impacts on real estate performance

Historically, income has driven 78% of long-term returns in global real estate investments.[1] While this share of returns tended to be lower during periods of higher growth and declining long-term interest rates, the current economic outlook is for growth to remain constrained and interest rates to remain elevated (relative to the prior decade). 2025 has seen significant shifts in global trading and economic growth patterns. Tariffs and trade wars have disrupted global trade flows, raising costs for importers and exporters alike, and contributing to slower growth in key economies, leading to a net decline in gross domestic product (GDP)[2]. Meanwhile regional conflicts continue to drive financial market volatility.

To translate how these geopolitical and macro-economic trends (a) could or would impact property investments, and (b) where we see greatest relative value and opportunity for value creation, we applied the following framework across the global real estate markets and sectors:

  • Potential impacts on structural growth trends, and the implications of this for sector level real estate net operating income (NOI) growth[3].
  • The potential impact on economic growth in different countries, and the implications of this for market-level real estate NOI growth.
  • The potential for real estate cap rate[4] compression to drive value growth.

As a result, we believe achieving the best real estate return potential, relative to the respective local market, will require a focus on property income growth and reliance on secular demand drivers that can mitigate an easing of the economic cycle. It also elevates the need to seek differentiated performance through market selection, and recent valuation evidence across our regions has shown an increasingly significant gap between top- and bottom-performing markets.

Trends potentially impacting NOI growth

Identifying income trends starts with examining multi-year structural growth patterns which drive the demand to occupy real estate, such as demographics, technology shifts, and sustainability requirements. However, the focus of this note is on more recent events, so we want to consider how these longer-term secular trends might be impacted by shorter- or medium-term changes, including:

  • Tariffs and trade flows: Companies around the world are starting to respond to US trade policy shifts. Overall, the impact is likely to be lower economic growth globally, though the wider implications on inflation and price levels are less clear.
  • Immigration and demographic changes: Many countries are seeing populist movements aimed at slowing immigration. Changes to the availability of foreign-born workers potentially place incremental pressure on those sectors of the economy which are typically most reliant on lower-skilled labor, including construction and manufacturing, as well as risking a “brain drain”, particularly in tech related sectors.
  • Housing affordability: Global house prices saw a strong upward trend during the last decade, fueled by low interest rates. While the recent correction in interest rates has seen prices slow in some countries, limited new supply means that most markets are seeing prices remain elevated, which combines with higher interest rates to compound the impact on the cost of home ownership.
  • Construction remains slow: Elevated interest rates and tight construction labor availability continue to weigh on developer confidence. We expect many markets to continue to see limited new supply growth until either these key constraints reverse, or local rents rise high enough to enable development to earn an appropriate risk-adjusted return.
  • Artificial intelligence (AI) and technology change: A number of markets are introducing policy measures to encourage further investment in this space, not least with respect to protecting and developing national or regional interests.
  • Climate risk: The populist policies we are seeing around the world often include a backlash against Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG), just at a time when significant climate events are causing significant increases to insurance costs in certain locations.

Trends potentially impacting pricing

After identifying the scope for income growth, we then consider the relative pricing of real estate investments. Again, we see the potential for pricing impacts from shorter-term factors such as:

  • Inflation and interest rates: Global inflation is unlikely to return sustainably below central banks' target levels, constraining how far central banks can cut base rates relative to recent history. In addition, efforts by central banks to shrink their balance sheets has consequential impacts on longer-term interest rates. Steeper yield curves[5] limit the scope for real estate cap rates to compress.
  • Cross border capital flows: Recent shifts in investor sentiment towards the US are driving a pullback by cross-border investors from the US. The sheer size gap between the US commercial real estate (CRE) market and others would mean that a reallocation of capital out of the US would likely have a significant market impact in the next most liquid global CRE markets.
  • Long-term return requirements: Ultra-low interest rates and global diversification created an environment of lower risk and therefore lower required returns. Unwinding this means investors seek higher required returns to compensate for the increase in risk.
  • The starting point matters: Sectors which are already seeing tight cap rates and hence lower income returns (driven by investor interest/capital flows) are less likely to see cap rate compression. Meanwhile some “out of favor” sectors, previously retail, now office, have initial higher income returns, as evidenced by recent relative changes in cap rates across our markets, which also enables investors to add accretive debt to enhance return potential, and as a result investor interest is gaining pace which improves the probability of seeing some yield compression in the near term.

Bringing this together to identify relative value

The discussion above is a very brief look at only a selection of the shorter-term fluctuations which are driving financial markets today. Such short-term dynamics do not preclude investment in long-term assets, but we believe successful strategies need to (a) be clear on the trends they are investing into, and (b) minimize exposures to “known unknowns.” Across both markets and real estate sectors, these trends will manifest differently and drive deviations in relative returns, hence our view that global investors need to increasingly consider the relative outlooks between different markets.

Considerations of where and how to invest in real estate need to consider the current relative pricing, which varies considerably by market, as we see below.

Current cap rate spreads versus history

Outside of China, spreads are below historic ranges but to varying degrees

Current cap rate spreads vs long-term ranges

At the time of this writing, market expectations are for the spot interest rates in most markets to reduce further. While this supports the argument that the current spot interest rates remain elevated, the spread of real estate cap rates over interest rates remains tight in some markets, even adjusting for expected rate cuts.

A normalization of these relative spreads over long-term interest rates, especially in the US, is important as the inversion of debt costs above cap rates has nullified the ability of financial leverage to enhance real estate equity returns, which in turn has sharply slowed the pace of transaction activity.

Furthermore, even in markets where cap rate spreads over interest rates remain below long-term trends, we still see good opportunities for real estate investment. However, in those markets where spreads are tight, we place a greater focus on real estate sectors with a higher starting cap rate, supported by a stable income stream; lower starting cap rates rely on stronger NOI growth to deliver equivalent returns, and therefore care is needed to select those assets and locations where this can be achieved.

Importantly, even in Europe, where cap rate spreads are back to within historic quartile ranges over interest rates, transaction volumes remain slow to recover as a result of cross-border capital flows remaining limited. As such, while we believe the relative pricing between different markets provides interesting opportunities for capital allocators, we remain of a view that real estate transaction volumes will see a broader global recovery across all regions.

Conclusion: Solving to maximize income returns and income growth

Our approach to real estate investment focuses on both optimizing income yield and income growth, and also minimizing our reliance on cap rate movement as the driver of returns. In seeking property income growth, we look for assets and opportunities which are either supported by long-term structural drivers of occupier demand, or where active asset management can drive improvements in the cash flows.

Geopolitical shifts continue to make the outlook volatile, but we believe that in this environment, a disciplined approach to asset selection and strong local knowledge will enable us to deliver relative outperformance potential.

Sector Considerations

Industrial:

  • Historically this has been a more cyclical sector; slower GDP growth and ongoing higher interest rates are likely to reduce demand in aggregate.
  • Expect leasing for logistics space to be shaped by shifts to global trade flows and how these impact volumes at key port and transit locations.
  • Moves towards more domestic manufacturing and improved certainty in supply chains will increase space demands in target markets over the mid-long term.

Residential:

  • Ongoing elevated interest rates are likely to result in a higher proportion of households remaining in rental housing as (a) the rental sector will continue to attract a higher share of new household formation than has seen historically, and (b) renters will remain renters for a longer period.
  • However, lower economic growth, and higher costs in certain markets, will mean that housing affordability is likely to remain an issue for many.

Office:

  • Historically this has been a more cyclical sector, though more recently demand has been shaped by shifting working patterns.
  • Demand is already bifurcating between Grade A[6] and secondary space; ongoing low levels of new supply will likely continue to drive this gap.
  • Technological advancements and AI are likely to see new jobs being created while others are rendered obsolete; the net impact on office demand is unclear at this stage.

Retail:

  • Slower economic growth and ongoing higher costs, including from ongoing elevated interest rates, is likely to limit the recovery in consumer confidence and therefore in retail spending.
  • In many markets, vacancy remains close to historic lows and supply is almost nonexistent.

Specialty sectors:

  • Data centers are the property sector poised to benefit most from AI investment, subject to constraints of power and water availability.
  • Ongoing limited housing supply drives an ongoing need to provide specialist accommodation for certain populations, e.g. student or senior housing.

Regional Considerations

North America:

  • Trump policy uncertainty continues to negatively impact business and consumer sentiment, elevating risks to growth.
  • Ultimate impacts of U.S. tariffs on inflation and growth remain unclear; Federal Reserve (Fed) easing[7] in “wait and see mode.'
  • Investors adopting a more cautious stance amid policy noise; y-o-y transaction volumes increased at a modest pace; yields remaining largely stable.
  • Expect both residential and commercial tenants to exercise additional caution in this environment. Deliveries continue to fall; starts remain limited.
  • What this means for investment execution:
  1. Slower revenue growth expectations require greater selectivity regarding locations and assets.
  2. Consider high cap rate sectors with stable incomes to hedge the potential for persistent higher interest rates.
  3. We believe real estate credit provides better relative value than equity, though price gains from last year have partially reduced the relative value gap.

Europe:

  • European recovery is ongoing; some concerns remain around manufacturing though investment in defense / infrastructure will at least partially offset this.
  • Inflation is moderating; the European Central Bank (ECB) and Bank of England (BofE) made rate cuts in 2024 and 2025, and the debate from here is on the speed of further adjustment from going forward.
  • Cap rates have been stable since March 2024, but capital demand is recovering slowly; we continue to regard cap rate compression as more of an upside case.
  • Real estate markets are likely to remain in risk-off mode, with limited new development supply. This will drive positive rental growth for Grade A space but weakening trends for lower quality or non-core locations.
  • Our key priority in 2025-26 is to exploit those opportunities that will arise from the current market repricing and dislocation. The key longer-term investment drivers we are focusing on are:
  1. Target demand driven by demographic changes and urbanization.
  2. Play the consumer demand for experiential leisure and travel.
  3. Non-traditional real estate sectors will continue to grow market share.

Asia-Pacific:

  • APAC economies see mixed impacts from new US tariffs. Australia remains relatively insulated. China benefits from front-loaded exports and a truce extension, though growth challenges persist. Japan and Korea negotiated partial tariff relief but still face substantial costs on key exports. Tariff-related noise is likely to continue.
  • Regional central banks adopt more measured actions. The Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) delivered an expected cut, while the Bank of Korea (BOK) and Bank of Japan (BOJ) held off on rate adjustments, signaling a wait-and-see stance.
  • We target opportunities driven by secular trends. Focus areas include demographic shifts (e.g. living sectors in Korea, Australia, China and Japan) and digitalization (e.g. data centers). We prioritize low-beta sectors[8] with wide supply-demand gaps and minimal geopolitical exposure, while also pursuing tactical plays from reset pricing and funding gaps in selected markets.

Defined Terms and Notes:

  1. Per MSCI Global Annual Property Index data, earliest data point January 1, 2001, latest data point December 31, 2024. The MSCI Global Annual Property Index is a market-weighted index that tracks the performance of directly held property investments across 25 global countries. Past performance does not guarantee future results. An investment cannot be made into an index.
  2. Gross domestic product (GDP) is a broad indicator of a region’s economic activity, measuring the monetary value of all the finished goods and services produced in that region over a specified period of time.
  3. Net operating income (NOI) growth is the increase in a property’s NOI over time. NOI measures the profitability of an asset or an investment after subtracting operating expenses from income.
  4. Capitalization rates (cap rates) is the quotient of a property’s net operating income divided by the property’s estimate value.
  5. The yield curve plots interest rates, at a set point in time, of bonds having equal credit quality but differing maturity dates.
  6. Grade A refers to higher quality buildings in their market and area, generally newer properties with desirable amenities and tenants.
  7. Easing typically involves lowering interest rates or increasing the money supply to stimulate the economy.
  8. Low beta sectors refer to sectors that are less volatile than the overall market.

Investment risks

The value of investments and any income will fluctuate (this may partly be the result of exchange rate fluctuations) and investors may not get back the full amount invested. Property and land can be difficult to sell, so investors may not be able to sell such investments when they want to. The value of property is generally a matter of an independent valuer’s opinion and may not be realised.

Generally, real estate assets are illiquid in nature. Although certain kinds of investments are expected to generate current income, the return of capital and the realization of gains, if any, from an investment will often occur upon the partial or complete disposition of such investment.

Investing in real estate typically involves a moderate to high degree of risk. The possibility of partial or total loss of capital will exist. Investing in commercial real estate assets involves certain risks, including but not limited to: tenants' inability to pay rent; increases in interest rates and lack of availability of financing; tenant turnover and vacancies; and changes in supply of or demand for similar property types in a given market.

Important information

This does not constitute a recommendation of any investment strategy or product for a particular investor. Investors should consult a financial professional before making any investment decisions if they are uncertain whether an investment is suitable for them.

Data as of August 2025, unless otherwise stated.

Where individuals or the business have expressed opinions, they are based on current market conditions, they may differ from those of other investment professionals and are subject to change without notice.

Forward-looking statements are not guarantees of future results. They involve risks, uncertainties and assumptions, there can be no assurance that actual results will not differ materially from expectations.

For U.S. audiences:

Invesco Distributors, Inc. is the U.S. distributor for Invesco’s retail products and private placement, and is an indirect, wholly owned subsidiary of Invesco Ltd.

For Canadian audiences:

Commissions, trailing commissions, management fees and expenses may all be associated with mutual fund investments. Mutual funds are not guaranteed, their values change frequently and past performance may not be repeated. Please read the simplified prospectus before investing. Copies are available from your advisor or from Invesco Canada Ltd.

Most references are U.S.-centric and may not apply to Canada. All data is USD, unless otherwise stated.

This document is issued in Canada by Invesco Canada Ltd. 16 York Street, Suite 1200, Toronto, Ontario, M5J 0E6. Invesco® and all associated trademarks are trademarks of Invesco Holding Company Limited, used under license.
